title-group.article-title: Filler‐based correction of tear trough depressions and eye bags in Japanese patients: A classification system and treatment algorithm

author-notes.corresp: *Correspondence Kiyoko Kato, Azabu Beauty Clinic, 2‐14‐11 Azabujuban, Minato‐ku, Tokyo 106‐0045, Japan. Email: kato@abcsalon.co.jp

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2022 The Authors. Journal of Cosmetic Dermatology published by Wiley Periodicals LLC.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/ License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited and is not used for commercial purposes.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Tear trough depressions are widely treated with fillers. However, it is questionable whether the same strategies can always be used for Caucasian and Japanese patients, owing to differences in bone and skin structures and epithelial thickness.

sec.title: Aims
sec.p: To assess a novel classification system and treatment algorithm for correcting tear trough depressions and eye bags in Japanese patients.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Tear trough depressions can be classified into three types according to the presence or absence of a “negative vector” based on retrusion of the maxilla relative to the anterior corneal surface, with or without eye‐bag protrusion when smiling. Type 1 patients (without the negative vector) can be treated simply by filling the depression in the tear trough and the palpebromalar groove region. Type 2 patients (with a negative vector and no eye‐bag protrusion on smiling) also require volume restoration in the mid‐cheek (deep medial cheek fat, suborbicularis oculi fat, and pre‐maxillary space); this not only enables volume recovery but also compensates for maxillary deficiency. Type 3 patients (with a negative vector and eye‐bag protrusion on smiling) require an additional step, based on placing a filler in front of the eye bags and pushing orbital fat backward.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Case presentations demonstrate the utility of the techniques involved.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: This system provides a simple and practical method for classifying and treating Japanese patients with tear trough depressions and eye bags. Further studies are warranted.

p.mixed-citation: KatoK, KajiwaraT, FuruyamaN, LiewS. Filler‐based correction of tear trough depressions and eye bags in Japanese patients: A classification system and treatment algorithm. J Cosmet Dermatol. 2023;22:439‐448. doi: 10.1111/jocd.1544936208029PMC10092177

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Japanese people are conservative by nature and typically prefer to keep their medical interactions private. Hence, in aesthetic medicine, they often do not want surgical procedures, with their associated long downtime and lack of reversibility. Instead, many patients show a strong preference for fillers, which have a short downtime and are potentially reversible. The benign safety profiles associated with injectables also fit well with this mindset.

Between 2010 and 2019, the estimated number of nonsurgical cosmetic procedures in Japan grew from 440 000 to 1.22 million, an increase of 174%. 1 , 2 This contrasts with a 31% decrease in surgical procedures over the same period. Among nonsurgical options, treatments based on injectable products—primarily botulinum toxin and hyaluronic acid (HA) fillers—rose from 270 000 to 460 000 per year, a 67% increase. 1 , 2

The move toward greater use of injectables may have been due in part to advances in product technology and injection techniques, and greater understanding of facial anatomy. These improvements have made it possible to treat more indications with injectables, which previously could only be managed surgically. One example is correction of the tear trough depression, particularly with eye bags. The traditional gold standard for eye bags is surgery, and this remains the case, but recently many Japanese patients have been successfully treated using filler injections.

We have developed a simple‐to‐use classification system and treatment algorithm for filler‐based correction of tear trough depressions and eye bags in Japanese patients. This method has been developed following review of more than 10 000 treatment sessions with injectables over the past 15 years to correct tear trough depressions in Asian patients (primarily Japanese). In this paper, we will describe key underlying anatomical features and our classification method, and illustrate the treatment of different patient types using case presentations.

sec.title: BONY STRUCTURAL AND SKIN CHARACTERISTICS OF JAPANESE PEOPLE
sec.p: As with many east Asians, Japanese faces are frequently characterized by a retruded central vertical third. In the midface, the maxilla is typically relatively recessive. Because the maxilla is the main bony structure forming the floor of the orbit, a recessive maxilla can give rise to prominent upper eyelids and an early appearance of eye bags. 3 These create a tired and aging look that often presents at an early age; unsurprisingly, this has led to a rise in young patients seeking treatment—even in their twenties. 4

With regard to skin characteristics, Japanese people typically have a thick dermis (like many other Asians), with less propensity for laxity than Caucasians. 5 For this reason, they often age better and over a longer time period than their Caucasians counterparts, 6 and sagging of the face tends to be milder among the elderly. However, early development of eye bags combined with better aging in the rest of the face can lead to a disharmony in facial appearance (Figure 1).

caption.p: Facial disharmony caused by eye bags in an older Japanese patient. A 63‐year‐old woman who presented with prominent eye bags (A). However, other signs of facial aging (such as sagginess) were mild in severity, creating a disharmony in her appearance. This is a common profile in Japan. She was treated only for dark circle depressions with eye bags—using Juvéderm Voluma in the deep fat component (0.5 ml on each side) and Humallagen in the tear trough and the palpebromalar groove region (1.0 ml on each side). 1 month later, she looked younger as a result (B). No other treatments were given.

sec.title: CLASSIFYING TEAR TROUGH DEPRESSIONS IN JAPANESE PATIENTS: TWO KEY INDICATORS
sec.p: Our method is based on classifying patients according to two key indicators: a facial vector and the impact of smiling on eye bag protrusion.

sec.title: Indicator 1: Facial vector
sec.p:
Several aspects of midface anatomy are central to our approach to treating tear trough depressions and eye bags in Japanese patients:
A vector defining the relationship between the anterior corneal surface and the malar eminence: As first described by Jelks, the anatomical characteristic in which the most anterior projection of the ocular globe lies behind the lower eyelid margin and the malar eminence may be classified as a “positive vector”; the opposite characteristic is a “negative vector” (Figure 2). 7 , 8 The exact location of the orbital margin is difficult to determine without radiographic imaging, such as computed tomography (CT). Hence, in practice, the vector is usually best evaluated based on the relative relationship between the anterior corneal surface and the malar eminence.
The relationship between this vector and eye bags: In individuals with a positive or neutral vector, orbital fat typically remains inside the orbit, and hence, eye bags are minimized. However, in patients with a negative vector, orbital fat lacks a floor and therefore protrudes forward to become eye bags (Figure 3).
This vector differs between Japanese and Caucasians: On average, in young Caucasian adults, the supraorbital rim projects 10 mm beyond, the orbital rim lies 3 mm behind, and the cheek prominence projects 2 mm beyond the anterior surface of the cornea. 9 There are no equivalent published studies in Japanese adults, but three‐dimensional CT imaging data from 10 patients (mean age: 21.1 years) found that the mean orbital fat protrusion from the orbital rim was 1.6 mm; 10 by contrast, a study of 14 Caucasians around the same age puts this distance at 0.89 mm. 11 Although these analyses were based on small numbers of subjects, we can infer that many Japanese may have eye bags and associated negative vectors even at a young age.

caption.p: Facial vector types. The vector defining the relationship between the anterior corneal surface and the malar eminence may be positive (left; cheek mass lying anterior to the cornea), neutral (center; cheek mass lying in the same plane as the cornea), or negative (right; cheek mass lying posterior to the cornea). This image is reproduced with permission from: Cohen AJ, Nguyen Burkat C, eds. Oculofacial, Orbital, and Lacrimal Surgery. 8

Relationship between the facial vector and eye bags. In an individual with a positive/neutral vector (A), orbital fat typically remains inside the orbit, and eye bags are usually less prominent. However, a negative vector (B) causes a partial absence of the floor of the orbital fat, which inevitably results in the appearance of eye bags. SOOF, suborbicularis oculi fat.

sec.p: In the treatment of Japanese patients, the presence or absence of a negative vector represents a potentially important practical classification criterion—one that is more objective than vague expressions of “volume loss in the midface” or “a retruded maxilla.” In our method, patients are first categorized based on having tear trough depressions with or without a negative vector when the face is static. Those with a negative vector usually have eye bags.

There are also cases of Japanese patients who develop a negative vector due to aging. It can be ascertained whether the negative vector was caused by aging or inherent anatomy (since birth) simply by asking the patient when the eye begs appeared. In either case, treatment will usually be the same because, as already described, Japanese skin is thick and age‐related sagging is typically minimal.

sec.title: Indicator 2: Protrusion of eye bags when smiling
sec.p: Eye bags are covered by the preseptal part of the palpebral component of the orbicularis oculi muscle (OOM). When the OOM is in active contraction during smiling, most individuals display an effacement of their eye bags. However, in a subgroup of patients, the eye bags become more prominent during smiling. This poses difficulties in achieving an optimal outcome with traditional injection techniques, and necessitates specific treatment.

sec.title: Classification of Japanese patients
sec.p:
Based on these two indicators, we can classify the tear trough depressions of Japanese patients into three different types (Table 1):
Type 1: Neutral or positive vector with dark circles.
Type 2: Eye bags with a negative vector, and no worsening of the eye bags when smiling.
Type 3: Eye bags with a negative vector, with worsening of the eye bags when smiling.

tr.td: Fill depression in the tear trough and the palpebromalar groove region

td.p: Correct negative vector (fill deep fat compartments)
Fill depression in the tear trough and the palpebromalar groove region
Correct negative vector (fill deep fat compartments)
Fill depression in the tear trough and the palpebromalar groove region
Fill in front of the orbital fat (preseptal space)

sec.p: From practical experience of more than 10 000 treatment sessions, we estimate that among individuals undergoing treatment of the tear trough depression (aged from the twenties to the seventies), about 45% are type 1, 40% are type 2, and 15% are type 3.

sec.title: TREATMENT BASED ON THIS CLASSIFICATION SYSTEM
sec.p: The treatment algorithm differs for each patient type. Various different fillers are used depending on specific requirements, but all injections are performed using a cannula.

Patients with particularly thin or inelastic skin are typically excluded, although in practice this is rare in Asia, even among older individuals. 5 In those with large eye bags, surgery may provide a better solution, but our method is still applicable and such patients remain eligible if they prefer a nonsurgical approach. Thus, most patients who meet the Type 1–3 criteria can be treated according to our algorithm (Table 1).

Case presentations are provided below. All of the individuals shown gave written consent for the use and analysis of their data. The guidelines of the Declaration of Helsinki were followed throughout. Guidance on clinical research in Japan exempts case series from ethical committee approval, and such approval was therefore not required for these retrospectively assessed case reports.

sec.title: Type 1
sec.p: Treatment of type 1 patients is relatively straightforward: fill the tear trough, and if the depression extends to the palpebromalar groove (PMG) region then also fill the PMG. We typically do this using human collagen (Humallagen, Cosmetic Medicine Enterprises Inc., Palm Harbor, FL, USA; available only in Japan). Alternatively, an HA filler can be used, as long as the product injected has the required physical properties; in particular, it should have low‐to‐medium elastic modulus (G') and low cohesivity (so that it is sufficiently spreadable to avoid overcorrection or the formation of lumps and bumps), as well as low water absorption (to avoid the Tyndall effect). 12 We use Teosyal® Redensity 2 (Teoxane Laboratories, Geneva, Switzerland) or Juvéderm® Volbella™ (Vycross 15 mg/ml HA [VYC‐15 L]; Allergan, Dublin, Ireland), owing to their longevity. If the patient wishes to have an injectable that is officially approved in Japan (as of September 2022), we use the latter.
Example results from a type 1 case are shown in Figure 4.

caption.p: Type 1: Treatment plan and results. A 36‐year‐old woman who presented with a type 1 defect (A, B). In profile view, she showed a positive vector (C). The treatment plan was based on filling the depression in the tear trough and palpebromalar groove region (shown in green in part (D)) using Humallagen (right side, 0.5 ml; left side, 0.5 ml). The result at 1‐month post‐treatment is shown in parts (E) and (F).

sec.title: Type 2
sec.p: The treatment of type 2 patients must begin with correction of the negative vector using an HA filler with medium or high G' (to resist shearing) and medium or high cohesivity (to resist compression and maintain projection). 12 We use Juvéderm® Voluma™ (Vycross 20 mg/ml HA [VYC‐20 L]; Allergan) but other well‐studied, approved fillers with appropriate physicochemical properties could be used instead. Injections are required across various areas in the deep fat compartments, including the deep medial cheek fat (DMCF), suborbicularis oculi fat (SOOF), and pre‐maxillary space. This improves the negative vector and resolves the lack of volume in the mid‐cheek.

In some rare cases—particularly those with a loose orbicularis retaining ligament (ORL) adhesion—the depression of the tear trough and the PMG region can be improved using this procedure alone. However, in most instances, depressions will still be visible, owing to the tethering effect of the ORL. 13 Hence, the tear troughs and the PMG region will need to be treated as already described for type 1 patients.
Sample results are shown in Figure 5.

caption.p: Type 2: Treatment plan and results. A 41‐year‐old woman who presented with a type 2 defect (A, B, C). In profile view, she showed a negative vector (D). When smiling, the eye bags were reduced (E). The treatment plan was based on (1) filling the deep fat compartments (DMCF, shown in blue in part (F); SOOF, yellow; pre‐maxillary space, red) using Juvéderm Voluma (1.0 ml per side); and (1) filling the depression in the tear trough and palpebromalar groove region (shown in green in part (F)) using Juvéderm Volbella* (1.5 ml per side). The result at 1‐month post‐treatment is shown in parts (G, H), and (I) DMCF, deep medial cheek fat; SOOF, suborbicularis oculi fat. *Juvéderm Volbella was used because this patient prioritized formal approval for use and sustained efficacy.

sec.title: Type 3
sec.p: This type of patient requires the use of a filler to bring the protruding orbital fat back to a posterior position. The first step is to restore the recessed area of the maxilla with an HA filler as already described for type 2 patients. Filler should then be injected into the depression of the tear trough and the PMG region as per type 1 defects. However, an additional requirement for type 3 patients is to inject a small amount of filler into the preseptal space. This is more easily reached using a lateral entry point rather than from below, and filler placement should be targeted slightly above the ORL. The preferred products are either human collagen (Humallagen) or an HA filler with low‐to‐medium G' and low cohesivity (Juvéderm Volbella or Redensity 2).

An illustration of the required filler placement is given in Figure 6, and sample results are provided in Figures 7, 8, and 9. The procedure for the case in Figure 9 can also be viewed in the accompanying video.

caption.p: Filler placement in a type 3 case. Filler is shown in green. SOOF, suborbicularis oculi fat.

Type 3: Treatment plan and results. A 44‐year‐old woman who presented with a type 3 defect, shown at rest (A, B, C) and when smiling ((D); demonstrating greater prominence of the eye bags). In profile view, she showed a negative vector (E). The treatment plan was based on (1) filling the deep fat compartments (DMCF, shown in blue in part (F); SOOF, yellow; pre‐maxillary space, red); (1) filling the depression in the tear trough and the palpebromalar groove region (shown in green in part (F)); and (3) placing a small amount of filler in the preseptal space (shown in purple in part (F)). To achieve this, Juvéderm Voluma was used in the deep fat component (0.5 ml per side) and Humallagen was injected into the tear trough, palpebromalar groove region, and preseptal space (right side, 0.8 ml; left side, 0.8 ml). The result at 1‐month post‐treatment is shown at rest (G, H, I) and when smiling (J). DMCF, deep medial cheek fat; SOOF, suborbicularis oculi fat.

Type 3: Treatment results. A 53‐year‐old woman who presented with a type 3 defect shown at rest (A, B) and when smiling (C; exhibiting eye bag prominence). Juvéderm Voluma was used in the deep fat component (0.5 ml per side), and Humallagen was injected into the tear trough, palpebromalar groove region, and preseptal space (right side, 0.6 ml; left side, 0.7 ml). The result at 1‐month post‐treatment is shown at rest (D, E) and when smiling (F).

Type 3: Treatment results. A 32‐year‐old woman who presented with a type 3 defect shown at rest (A, B) and when smiling ((C); exhibiting eye bag prominence). She was treated in the deep fat component using Juvéderm Voluma (0.5 ml per side), and in the tear trough, palpebromalar groove region, and preseptal space using Juvéderm Volbella (0.3 ml) on the right side and Humallagen (0.4 ml) on the left side. The result 1‐month post‐treatment is shown at rest (D, E) and when smiling (F), demonstrating reduced prominence of the eye bags. The procedure with this patient can be viewed in the accompanying video. Different products were used in the right and left side to highlight differences in the injection methods.

sec.p: For each of the three patient types, procedure‐related adverse events are typically mild, but may include bruising, swelling, redness, and pain. In most cases, these resolve within a few days.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: A wide variety of anatomical factors underlie the formation of tear trough depressions, including the skin, soft tissue, ligament status, and bone condition. Thus, classification of defects in this area can be complex. Various proposals have been made previously, for example by Hirmand, 14 Lee, 15 and Peng. 16

We have developed a simple, practical, experience‐based method for classifying tear trough depressions in Japanese patients. The central element of our system is the vector that defines the relative relationship between the anterior corneal surface and the malar eminence. To the best of our knowledge, there are no previously published reports emphasizing the relevance of this vector for treating tear trough depressions.

When a Caucasian patient has a negative vector, it is usually due to the aging process, 11 and is caused by bony atrophic change occurring medially and posteriorly. 17 Because these individuals are typically older, they are likely to shown significant aging of the skin by the time the negative vector appears, and treatment therefore cannot be based on simple correction of the vector. By contrast, for many Japanese (and other Asians), the negative vector exists from birth and worsens as a result of aging‐related bone atrophy. Hence, tear trough depressions with eye bags often manifest even at a young age. Furthermore, Asian skin has a thicker and more compact dermis compared with Caucasians, 5 possibly because it has larger and more nucleated fibroblasts and smaller collagen fiber bundles. 18

Overall, the reduced correlation of the negative vector with aging in Japanese patients—alongside thicker and less saggy skin—means that simple treatment of the negative vector can be sufficient. This is less likely to be the case with Caucasians. To improve the negative vector, we inject an HA filler into the mid‐cheek. The importance of volumizing this area has been described previously, 19 , 20 although there the aim was primarily to counter aging‐related volume loss. In the present method, volumizing of the mid‐cheek has an additional intention. Specifically, injections in the DMCF, medial SOOF, and pre‐maxillary space play a key role in compensating for deficiency of the maxilla and for the missing floor of the orbital fat.

The SOOF is a closed space with the zygomatic cutaneous ligament below, OOM in front, and ORL above (the ORL being the entire ligament of the tear trough and the PMG region). 21 , 22 Hence, injecting an HA filler in this area will inevitably raise the ORL, potentially replacing the maxilla deficit. The pre‐maxillary space is not completely closed, but it is possible to compensate for the maxilla defect by placing a filler directly beneath the OOM.

The second key factor in our classification system is the impact of smiling. We first noted the phenomenon of eye bag protrusion during smiling several years ago, after a patient commented that her dark circle depressions were not improved when she smiled. Since then, we have observed that around 15% of patients with eye bags belong to this type. Some are aware of it themselves; many others are not.

It is still unclear why this protrusion occurs. Peng has observed that some patients show prominence of orbital fat in the “Squint test,” 16 which may be the same phenomenon. It is possible that orbital fat herniates from fenestrations in the OOM, 23 but such fenestrations are aging‐related, and hence, this effect cannot explain protrusion in younger patients. We believe that individuals whose ORL attaches to the bone and skin very tightly present with worsening eye bags when smiling. Owing to strong adhesion of the ORL to the bone, contraction of the orbital component of the OOM is stopped at the tear trough and the PMG region even when the patient smiles; as a result, the force of muscle contraction in the preseptal part of the palpebral component of the OOM may be weak. Filler placement in the preseptal space reinforces the weakness of the muscle and can bring the orbital fat backward.

It has been shown elsewhere that injection of the lateral part of the orbital can strengthen lower eyelid support and reduce the required injection volume in the tear trough. 24 However, Asian skulls are typically wider than in Caucasians, with greater bitemporal, bizygomatic, and bigonial width. 4 Adding volume to the zygoma through treatment outside the orbital risks further accentuating its width, creating a morphologically unbalanced appearance. Thus, it is not included within our algorithm.

We use four different products: an HA filler (Juvéderm Voluma) for correction of a negative vector, and human collagen (Humallagen) or an HA filler (Redensity 2 or Juvéderm Volbella) for improving tear trough and PMG depressions and eye bag protrusion. Human collagen has the advantage that it can be used both on the bone and on the very surface of the skin because of its high consistency with the skin—collagen makes up around 70% of its composition. In addition, the collagen product does not require allergy testing (due to its human origin), does not absorb water, is easy to mold, and carries no risk of the Tyndall effect. This is important given that slight depressions often remain after deeper injections have been completed, necessitating filler placement directly under the skin to create a perfectly smooth surface.

The main disadvantage of collagen is its short duration of effect—typically only around 4 months. In patients who are concerned about this, or who are found to degrade the human collagen product particularly rapidly, HA is a good alternative. It must be carefully injected as deep as possible using a microdroplet technique because it can absorb water, which may lead to the Tyndall effect. From a practical perspective, when using HA, it may be desirable to start with a small amount and add more later. With collagen, after the swelling subsides, patients sometimes feel that the amount injected is insufficient and wish to add more. Thus, with either type of filler, retouching may be necessary to achieve a perfectly flat surface.
It should be noted that Humallagen has not been approved by the US Food and Drug Administration, and its use outside of Japan may be limited.

sec.title: CONCLUSIONS
sec.p: Various factors underlie the development of tear trough depressions and eye bags, and it is essential that these are appraised at an individual level. However, for Japanese patients, assessment and treatment planning can be made relatively simple using the classification system proposed here—based on evaluation of a facial vector (defining the relationship between the anterior corneal surface and the malar eminence) and consideration of the effect of smiling on eye bag protrusion. We have demonstrated the utility of our algorithm using patient case studies; verification will be required in a larger cohort. The method has so far been used primarily with Japanese patients, but other east Asians typically have similar skeletal and skin anatomy, and hence, it should be broadly applicable. However, further research may be necessary to confirm this. Given that Caucasian patients with eye bags and a negative vector are typically older and have relatively more skin excess, it is questionable whether the same techniques can be applied. Nonetheless, it would be valuable to assess the feasibility of using this method with younger patients from other racial groups, including Caucasians.
Overall, the present method offers a simple and practical tool for classifying and treating Japanese patients with tear trough depressions and eye bags. Further study is warranted.

sec.title: AUTHOR CONTRIBUTIONS
sec.p: Dr Kiyoko Kato, Dr Nobutaka Furuyama, and Dr Steven Liew conceived the classification system and treatment algorithm. Dr Kiyoko Kato and Dr Tomoe Kajiwara developed its utility in clinical practice. All authors were involved in drafting the manuscript, and all approved the final version.

sec.title: FUNDING INFORMATION
sec.p: The authors report no outside funding to disclose.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no conflicts of interest to disclose.

sec.title: ETHICS STATEMENT
sec.p: The authors confirm that the ethical policies of the journal, as noted on the journal's author guidelines page, have been adhered to. No ethical approval was required as no original research data are provided. The guidelines of the Declaration of Helsinki were followed throughout.

sec.title: PHOTO CONSENT
sec.p: Informed consent was obtained from all patients shown in the figures.

caption.p: Click here for additional data file.

ack.title: ACKNOWLEDGMENTS
ack.p: We thank Dr Akiko Nomura and Dr Timothy Ryder for their help in refining the English in this paper. We also acknowledge BlueGiraffe Inc. for their assistance in creating facial layer images.

sec.title: DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
